topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the open-plan office seemed to hum a malicious tune today. I kept glancing over my shoulder, a nervous habit I hadn't shaken. It started subtly—a misplaced file, a slightly delayed email response—but then I saw the numbers. A casual glance at the shared project metrics revealed a stark, undeniable gap between my compensation and that of Alex, the new junior analyst. My jaw tightened, and I felt a sudden, cold clench in my stomach. Every shared glance felt like an accusation. I started double-checking my own spreadsheets, my fingers trembling slightly over the keyboard, convinced that some hidden mechanism was at play, some elaborate scheme designed to keep me small. I made sure to keep my interactions with Alex brief, clipped, and utterly professional, my voice pitched a little too high when I spoke to him.

[story 2]

I spent the entire lunch break staring at my reflection in the darkened glass of the elevator doors. My hands were slick with sweat, and I could feel the rapid drumming of my pulse against my ribs. It wasn't just the salary discrepancy; it was the way Alex navigated the office—the easy confidence, the way senior managers seemed to gravitate toward him. I started cataloging every interaction, dissecting every casual conversation, searching for the telltale sign, the slip-up that would prove my suspicion correct. When I caught myself looking at his desk too long, I would snap my gaze away instantly, my breath catching in my throat. I started taking meticulous notes on everything, compiling a mental dossier, convinced that if I just observed closely enough, the truth would burst into the open, and I would be the first to see it.

[story 3]

The air in the breakroom felt thick, suffocating. I kept re-reading the pay scale documentation, the fine print blurring before my eyes. It was a certainty now, a heavy stone lodged in my chest. Every time Alex entered the room, I would subtly shift my position, angling my body so I could observe his movements from the corner of my eye. His laughter sounded too bright, too unearned. I found myself replaying past conversations in my head, trying to find the moment where the disparity became undeniable. My hands began to shake when I reached for my coffee mug, the ceramic rattling slightly. I started avoiding shared workspaces, retreating to my cubicle like a hermit, convinced that proximity was inviting danger, that if I stayed still, I might somehow avoid the inevitable revelation.
